title: Resentment as a slow tax
subtitle: What long-held resentment actually costs, and why forgiveness is not about the other person
excerpt: Resentment is not a neutral state of holding ground. It has ongoing costs, cognitive, emotional, and physical, that accumulate across time. This reading explains what the research finds about those costs, and what forgiveness actually means when the person who wronged you doesn't deserve it.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from research on forgiveness, resentment, and the psychophysiology of holding grievance. No single source. Framing original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: When carrying a long-held resentment and the argument for holding it has started to cost more than it provides

content.text: Resentment feels like holding ground, like refusing to let the person who wronged you off the hook. The research on what resentment actually does over time suggests a different picture: it is an ongoing tax, paid by the person carrying it, not the person who caused it.

Studies on long-held resentment find higher stress hormones, higher heart rate, and greater blood pressure compared to people who have released comparable grievances. Replaying the original wrong activates the same stress response as the original event. Doing it repeatedly compounds the cost.

The cognitive cost runs alongside the physical one. Chronic resentment occupies working memory. It shows up as intrusive thoughts, as conversations rehearsed in the shower, as the same material revisited across years. The bandwidth taken by the resentment is bandwidth not available for other things.

Forgiveness is not condoning what happened. It is not a verdict on whether the other person was wrong. It is not reconciliation or resumed contact or the erasure of consequences. Forgiveness research defines it as the internal shift in which the wronged person reduces or releases negative affect toward the person who harmed them. This is for the wronged person's benefit.
This distinction matters. The person who wronged you may be entirely, permanently wrong. Releasing the resentment doesn't change that. The release is about what you carry, not what they deserve.
Drawn from research on forgiveness and long-held resentment. No single source. Framing by homemade.education.
